content.full_text:
FPD & C

December 9, 1990 DEC 1 0 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Request for Confirming Letter

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am an elected member of the Board of Directors of the
San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District ("BART"), a rapid
transit district existing under Sections 28,500 et seq. of the
California Public Utilities Code. I have been a board member
since 1974 and represent BART District No. 6 in southern Alameda
County.

I am the owner of a claims adjusting firm, John Glenn
Adjusters ("Adjusters") which operates in the San Francisco Bay
Area. Adjusters provides services to several transit providers
in the San Francisco Bay Area, including San Mateo County
Transit, Central Contra Costa Transit Authority, the Golden Gate
Bridge District and the Santa Clara County Transit District.
Each of these transit companies has paid more than $250 in fees
for services to Adjusters in the past twelve months. Thus, I
consider that each of the transit companies is a "source of
income" to me as defined in the Political Reform Act and its
regulations. Adjusters' services consist of evaluating and
negotiating settlement of personal injury and property damages
claims asserted by members of the public. In large part, these
claims are related to motor vehicle collisions involving buses
operated by the various transit companies. Claimants are usually
third parties, although bus riders comprise a significant number
of claimants.

From time to time matters come before the BART Board of
Directors which may indirectly touch upon, in some way, the
business activities of a particular transit company which is a
source of income to me. My primary question concerns application
of the tests of foreseeability with respect to determining
whether there is a material financial affect on a source of
income. I would appreciate it if your response is in enough
detail to enable me to deal with particular questions which are
bound to arise in the future as a result of BART's committed and
proposed extension programs.

California FPPC
Re: Confirming Letter

- 2 -

December

, 1990

Potentially, any new or extended BART rail line could
have an impact on any bus company operating in the vicinity of
the rail line. If the bus services were competing with the rail
services, there might be a detrimental impact at least on the
particular bus route or routes where it could not compete. If
the bus company decided to operate service which was complimentary to the rail transit service, by providing feeder service
to the rail stations, for example, the bus company's business
might improve.

Two recent issues which came before the BART Board of
Directors will illustrate, I hope, the nature of the problem. On
November 1, 1990 one of my fellow directors proposed that BART
subsidize certain bus service in the community of Orinda. The
existing bus service provider, the Central Contra Costa Transit
Authority (CCCTA), would have been asked to permit BART riders to
ride from and from the BART station. BART would have, in some
manner, paid the amount of the fare for those riders. The
subsidy proposal was intended to entice casual car pool riders
not to park cars at the Orinda BART station. The proposal
involved only two months of service, on two of the CCCTA's bus
lines, and would have cost BART $12,000.

CCCTA is a non-profit, public entity which operates
buses on about 25 routes, primarly in Contra Costa County. As
noted above, it is a source of income to me. In its fiscal year
1989, the CCCTA appears to have had gross annual receipts in
excess of $10,000,000, but less than $100,000,000.1/ CCCTA did
not initiate or participate in this proposition, and there was
not contract before the Board of Directors.

I consulted BART's General Counsel on this matter and,
given the proposed $12,000 expenditure, to be made in the next
two months, and using the yardstick of Section 18702.5(c) of the
regulations it was clear that this would not have a material
financial affect on CCCTA. Accordingly, I voted on the
question. On similar matters, when the proposed action would
have an effect within the reasonably foreseeable future, and the
materiality of the financial affect can be measured, I would
expect to follow the same analytical process. Please confirm
that this is appropriate, particularly the application of §
18702.5(c).

On the other hand, in October 1990 the Board proposed
to consider moving an undefined project for transit in the San
Ramon corridor (a geographic area running approximately 20 miles

1/ Total revenues for transit services fiscal year 1989 -
$12,733,000. Source "The County Connection 9-Year Short-Range
Transit Plan, September 1990." Prepared by Central Contra Costa
Transit Authority.

California FPPC
Re: Confirming Letter

- 3 -

December

, 1990

between Pleasanton and Walnut Creek and generally following the
route of Interstate 680) from a Stage III project to a Stage II
project.

By way of background, the BART extension program is
divided into three stages (copy attached). Stage I extension
projects are likely to be completed, although full funding is not
available at this time, and the completion date for the various
lines are between 1995 and 2000. The Stage II projects, on the
other hand, are less defined, unscheduled and unfunded. Finally,
Stage III projects are largely undefined. In this regard, basic
decisions regarding the possible San Ramon corridor line have not
even been made. Major determinations, such as whether a heavy
rail (such as BART), or a light rail/trolley line should be used,
the location of the route, and the number of stations are wholly
up in the air. In my opinion, the only effect of the change in
staging for the San Ramon corridor project would be to notify the
public that BART was indeed interested in providing rail transit
in the corridor. That decision, however, would not bring the
project closer to fruition.2/

However, because of the source of income relationship
with CCCTA, which operates one of its 25 bus routes in the San
Ramon corridor, I requested clearance from BART's General
Counsel, as has been my practice.

The General Counsel suggested that I seek clarification
from the Commission on whether or not the possible financial
affect on CCCTA is foreseeable given the preliminary status of
this proposed project. Pending your response, I am not participating in discussions involving this matter.

In an attempt to analyze the materiality of any affect
on CCCTA, the General Counsel requested that the District's
planning staff research the estimated financial impact on
CCCTA. However, in order to consider this request the planning
staff found it necessary to make the following assumptions which
I believe are far too speculative to be foreseeable:

1. A San Ramon Valley light rail line (a non-BART type
system to be operated by BART) would operate from Walnut Creek
BART co Dublin with its southern terminal near the proposed BART
station near the former Southern Pacific San Ramon Branch intersection with Interstate 580 in Dublin.

_____________________________
2/ It is important to note that listing on the extension staging
policy does not constitute project approval by BART. This does
not occur until the EIR process is completed. This generally
does not occur until a proposed project reaches Stage I. There
has been no evironmental work on the San Ramon corridor project
by BART.

California FPPC
Re: Confirming Letter

- 4 -

December , 1990

2. Rail line operation would begin in the near future.

3. Light rail stations would be located in Alamo,
Danville, and Bishop Ranch. Stations would each have minimal
parking facilities due to limited land availability.

4. Current costs and equipment are used for
calculations.

5. CCCTA would operate two feeder bus routes to serve
the light rail system in place of their existing 121 Route, which
would be discontinued.

Route A would operate on a circular route from
Alamo via Stone Valley Road and Diablo Road to Danville then
return back to Alamo via Danville Boulevard. Weekday peak period
service would be provided at 15-minute headways while midday
service would operate every hour. Peak period service would
require two buses while one bus could handle the midday. Thirty-
minute Saturday service would be provided for nine hours. Two,
peak period and one midday bus would be utilized on this route.

Route B would operate on a circular route from
Bishop Ranch via Bollinger Canyon Road, Crow Canyon Road, and San
Ramon Valley Boulevard back to Bishop Ranch.

6. Estimated passenger levels are based on current
population characteristics.

Please note the basic assumption is that light rail
operations will begin in the near future. In fact, operations
will probably not start for 15 - 25 years. In my opinion,
current population characteristics would not support even a light
rail line at this time.

The Planning Department concluded, based on the
assumptions above, that:

1. CCCTA would receive an increase in gross annual
receipts of approximately $18,000.

2. CCCTA would have a decrease in expenses of
approximately $280,000.

3. CCCTA would save approximately $165,000 in assets
because one less bus would be required.

In my view, unlike the earlier example of providing a
subsidy for bus riders coming to the Orinda BART Station, the
financial impact on CCCTA of the proposed San Ramon corridor
staging decision is not reasonably foreseeable. I do not believe
that the assumptions that need to be made to even attempt to

California FPPC
Re: Confirming Letter

- 5 -

December , 1990

measure materiality in the distant future are appropriate at this
time.

Therefore, with regard to the San Ramon corridor
staging decision, I request answers to the following:

1.    Is a financial effect on CCCTA reasonably fore-
seeable from a decision to elevate the San Ramon corridor project
from Stage III to Stage II of BART's staging policy, notwith-
standing the fact that the earliest start of any construction is
probably 10 to 20 years in the future and subject to numerous
contingencies in the planning and approval process, including
obtaining financing and the EIR process?

2.    Is the materiality of any financial effect fore-
seeable from a decision to elevate the San Ramon corridor project
from Stage III to Stage II of BART's policy, notwithstanding the
fact that any estimate of future effect will be projecting
commencement of operations 15 to 25 years in the future and be
based on assumptions and predictions which are conjectural?

3.    If the answers to 1 and 2 are affirmative, how can
you reconcile those answers with your advice to me in August 1988
(No. A-88-240) and your advice to former BART Director Robert
Allen in March 1982 (No. 82-1-004)?

I will be pleased to provide any further information
you require.  Feel free to call or, if you prefer, I am willing
to meet with your staff and provide documentation and
information.

Yours very truly,

John Glenn
BART Director,
District No. 6

Attachment

BART EXTENSION STAGING POLICY ¹

PHASE | INSIDE CURRENT DISTRICT OR UNDER FUNDING AGREEMENTS
---|---
I. | North Concord-West Pittsburg ²
 | Irvington-Warm Springs ³
 | Castro Valley - Dublin ³⁴
 | MUNI Metro Extension Project ⁴
 | Colma-Tanforan-San Francisco Airport ⁵
II. | Pittsburg-West Antioch-East Antioch
 | Pleasanton-West Livermore-East Livermore ³
 | San Francisco ⁶
 | San Pablo-Hilltop
 | Oakland Airport Connector ⁷
III. | San Francisco ⁶
 | Pinole-Hercules/Rodeo-Crockett
 | San Ramon Corridor

NOTES:

1. The several segments shown under each Roman numeral are understood to be implemented concurrently, to the extent that funding is available. BART will be the operator for any new heavy or light rail transit starts or extensions within the three BART counties.

2. To be extended east beyond West Pittsburg as funding permits, per SB 1715 of 1988.

3. Third station may be constructed only with funds additional to those identified in MTC Resolution 1876 (as revised in 1989).

4. The San Francisco Project is identified through coordination with the City and County of San Francisco as the MUNI Metro Extension to the CALTRAIN Depot South of Market.

5. Agreement of February 28, 1990 with SamTrans to proceed with SFO extension, subject to BART project approval.

6. Specific San Francisco Project to be identified through coordination with the City and County of San Francisco. Section 29034.5 of the California Public Utilities Code lists an extension of District services and facilities to the northwest section of the City and County of San Francisco as a District service commitment.

7. A people-mover, or some other mode of travel, to the Oakland Airport to be established before or at the same time as an extension of BART to the San Francisco Airport.

8. Funding from Proposition 116 shall not be allocated to the Warm Springs Extension (WSX) until funding for the Dublin-Pleasanton Extension has been guaranteed.

PHASE | OUTSIDE CURRENT DISTRICT ⁹
---|---
I. | Milpitas
II. | Millbrae-Menlo Park
 | Milpitas-San Jose
III. | Menlo Park-San Jose

9. Subject to a satisfactory cost-sharing arrangement with San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties and project approval by BART. Pursuant to Section 29034.5 of the California Public Utilities Code, only non-District funds may be spent by the District for the purpose of extending services and facilities outside of District's January 1, 1971 boundaries until the District meets specified service commitments within the 1971 boundaries.

Rev. 10/25/90

embedding.first_500_words: FPD & C December 9, 1990 DEC 1 0 1990 Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel California Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Re: Request for Confirming Letter Dear Mr. Hallabrin: I am an elected member of the Board of Directors of the San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District ("BART"), a rapid transit district existing under Sections 28,500 et seq. of the California Public Utilities Code. I have been a board member since 1974 and represent BART District No. 6 in southern Alameda County. I am the owner of a claims adjusting firm, John Glenn Adjusters ("Adjusters") which operates in the San Francisco Bay Area. Adjusters provides services to several transit providers in the San Francisco Bay Area, including San Mateo County Transit, Central Contra Costa Transit Authority, the Golden Gate Bridge District and the Santa Clara County Transit District. Each of these transit companies has paid more than $250 in fees for services to Adjusters in the past twelve months. Thus, I consider that each of the transit companies is a "source of income" to me as defined in the Political Reform Act and its regulations. Adjusters' services consist of evaluating and negotiating settlement of personal injury and property damages claims asserted by members of the public. In large part, these claims are related to motor vehicle collisions involving buses operated by the various transit companies. Claimants are usually third parties, although bus riders comprise a significant number of claimants. From time to time matters come before the BART Board of Directors which may indirectly touch upon, in some way, the business activities of a particular transit company which is a source of income to me. My primary question concerns application of the tests of foreseeability with respect to determining whether there is a material financial affect on a source of income. I would appreciate it if your response is in enough detail to enable me to deal with particular questions which are bound to arise in the future as a result of BART's committed and proposed extension programs. California FPPC Re: Confirming Letter - 2 - December , 1990 Potentially, any new or extended BART rail line could have an impact on any bus company operating in the vicinity of the rail line. If the bus services were competing with the rail services, there might be a detrimental impact at least on the particular bus route or routes where it could not compete. If the bus company decided to operate service which was complimentary to the rail transit service, by providing feeder service to the rail stations, for example, the bus company's business might improve. Two recent issues which came before the BART Board of Directors will illustrate, I hope, the nature of the problem. On November 1, 1990 one of my fellow directors proposed that BART subsidize certain bus service in the community of Orinda. The existing bus service provider, the Central Contra Costa Transit Authority (CCCTA), would
